paper_title: The late Pleistocene origin of the Italian and Maltese populations of Potamon fluviatile (Malacostraca: Decapoda): insights from an expanded sampling of molecular data

methods.description: Collection of novel specimens and preservation of tissue samples for genetic analysis.

steps.description: In total, 40 novel specimens (1-7 per site; see Table I) were analysed, taking one leg from each individual, and releasing them back in the same sampling site where they were collected.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect biological samples (legs) from specimens

steps.description: Sampled legs were fixed in situ in 90% ethanol.
steps.searchable_summary: Preserve biological samples in ethanol

methods.description: Extraction of genomic DNA from preserved muscle tissue.

steps.description: Genomic DNA was extracted from the muscle tissue of each leg using the Genomic DNA Extraction Kit (RBC BioScience) following the manufacturer's protocol.
steps.searchable_summary: Extract genomic DNA from tissue using a commercial kit

methods.description: Amplification of a 502-bp fragment of the cytochrome oxidase subunit I (COI) gene using Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR).

steps.description: The PCR mix consisted of 18.05 µL double-distilled water, 2.5 µL Buffer 10X including 15 mM MgCl 2 solution, 0.25 µL dNTPs (10 mM of each), 0.9 µL of each primer (10 µM), 0.4 µL Taq Polymerase 5 u/µL and 2 µL of DNA template, for a total volume of 25 µL.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare PCR reaction mix

steps.description: The thermal cycle (35 cycles; denaturing 95°C for 45 s/annealing 46°C for 45 s/extension 72°C for 50 s) was conducted with the primers COL6b (5ʹ-ACA AAT CAT AAA GAT ATY GG-3ʹ) and COH6 (5ʹ-TAD ACT TCD GGR TGD CCA AAR AAY CA-3ʹ), described by Schubart and Huber (2006).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform PCR thermal cycling

methods.description: Validation of PCR product size and purity using gel electrophoresis, followed by purification and sequencing of successful reactions.

steps.description: After PCR, 5 µL of each PCR product were separated by electrophoresis on a 2% agarose gel at 90 V for 20 minutes and visualised with a UV Transilluminator.
steps.searchable_summary: Validate PCR product size using gel electrophoresis
outputs: Gel image showing PCR product bands

steps.description: When PCR products showed a clear and single band of the correct expected length, they were purified using the Exo-SAP-IT® kit (Affymetrix USB) and sequenced by Macrogen Inc. (Seoul, South Korea) with ABI 3130xL (Applied Biosystems) sequencer.
steps.searchable_summary: Purify and sequence validated PCR products

steps.description: The forward primers were used for direct sequencing of the PCR product.
steps.searchable_summary: Use forward primers for sequencing

methods.description: Acquisition of public sequence data and processing of both novel and public sequences for analysis.

steps.description: In addition, 21 haplotype sequences, corresponding to 104 individuals collected throughout the known distribution range of the species, were downloaded from GenBank (accession numbers: EU908226-EU908246) and analysed along with our sequences.
steps.searchable_summary: Download sequence data from GenBank

steps.description: The quality of the obtained chromatograms was checked through the measurement of their Phred scores (Richterich 1998).
steps.searchable_summary: Check sequence quality using Phred scores

steps.description: Only sequences with continuous reads of high-quality bases Quality Value scores ((QV) > 20) were used.
steps.searchable_summary: Filter sequences based on quality scores

steps.description: Sequences were analysed and manually proofread with the DNA sequencing Upon aligning our novel sequences with those downloaded from GenBank, we had to trim out 35 bp from the sequences downloaded from GenBank, which were originally 537 bp long.
steps.searchable_summary: Proofread, align, and trim sequence data

methods.description: Plotting the geographical locations of sampling sites on a map.

steps.description: The map of the sampling sites (see Figure 1) was plotted using the QGIS software v. 2.18.2 (http://www.qgis.org).
steps.searchable_summary: Plot sampling site locations on a map using GIS software

methods.description: Construction of a haplotype network to visualize relationships among identified haplotypes.

steps.description: A haplotype network for the identified sequences was constructed through the software Popart (v. 1.7, http://popart.otago.ac.nz), using the median-joining method (Bandelt et al. 1999) (Figure 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Construct a haplotype network using the median-joining method

methods.description: Analysis of molecular variance (AMOVA) to detect population differentiation based on predefined geographical groups.

steps.description: We partitioned a priori the sampling sites into three geographical groups: (i) Balkans; (ii) Central Italy; and (iii) southern peninsular Italy, Sicily, and the Maltese Islands (hereafter referred to as "ISM") in order to detect population differentiation through the analysis of their molecular variance (AMOVA; Excoffier et al. 1992).
steps.searchable_summary: Define geographical groups for population analysis

steps.description: AMOVA, neutrality tests and mismatch analysis were performed with the software Arlequin (v. 3.5.2.2, Excoffier & Lischer 2010).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Analysis of Molecular Variance (AMOVA)
outputs: AMOVA results (e.g., variance components, Fst statistics)

methods.description: Evaluation of population demography using neutrality tests and mismatch distribution analysis.

steps.description: Tajima's D test and Fu's FS test (Tajima 1989;Fu 1997) were performed in order to evaluate the population demography of this species.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Tajima's D and Fu's FS neutrality tests

steps.description: A mismatch distribution analysis was performed to estimate the parameters of demographic expansion.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform mismatch distribution analysis
outputs: Mismatch distribution parameters (e.g., tau, theta0, theta1)

steps.description: AMOVA, neutrality tests and mismatch analysis were performed with the software Arlequin (v. 3.5.2.2, Excoffier & Lischer 2010).
steps.searchable_summary: Execute analyses using Arlequin software

methods.description: Computation of standard genetic diversity indices: haplotype diversity and nucleotide diversity.

steps.description: In addition, haplotype diversity and nucleotide diversity were computed with the software DnaSP v. 5 (Librado & Rozas 2009).
steps.searchable_summary: Compute haplotype diversity

steps.description: In addition, haplotype diversity and nucleotide diversity were computed with the software DnaSP v. 5 (Librado & Rozas 2009).
steps.searchable_summary: Compute nucleotide diversity

methods.description: Statistical testing for significant differences in haplotype diversity between predefined geographical groups.

steps.description: In order to estimate the significance of the pair-wise differences in haplotype diversity between the three geographical groups, we used a series of functions, developed by Thomas et al. (2002) and available at http://www.ucl.ac.uk/macelab/resources/software ("TEST_h_DIFF"), through the statistical software "R" (v. 3.2.1, https://www.Rproject.org).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform pair-wise statistical tests for haplotype diversity differences between groups using R
inputs: Haplotype diversity values per geographical group
outputs: Statistical test results (e.g., p-values) for pair-wise comparisons

methods.method_name: Past Population Demography Inference (Bayesian Skyline Plot)
methods.description: Inference of past population size changes over time using a Bayesian skyline plot model.

steps.description: The choice of the correct evolutionary model was conducted using jModelTest (v. 2.0, Guindon & Gascuel 2003;Darriba et al. 2012) according to the Akaike information criterion (AIC; Akaike 1974).
steps.searchable_summary: Select evolutionary model using jModelTest and AIC

steps.description: Past population demography was inferred using a Bayesian skyline plot model (Drummond et al. 2005), as implemented in BEAST v. 1.8.0 (Drummond et al. 2012) and visualised in TRACER v. 1.6 (Rambaut et al. 2014). The coalescent approach uses standard Monte Carlo Markov chain (MCMC) sampling procedures to estimate a posterior distribution of gene genealogies and population parameters under a TN93 + I substitution model (Tamura & Nei 1993).
steps.searchable_summary: Set up Bayesian skyline plot model in BEAST

steps.description: A lognormal model that relaxes the molecular clock hypothesis was used (Drummond et al. 2006).
steps.searchable_summary: Specify relaxed molecular clock model
outputs: BEAST analysis configuration with relaxed clock

steps.description: The hyper-parameter "m" (number of grouped intervals) was set to 10.
steps.searchable_summary: Set Bayesian skyline plot hyperparameter 'm'

steps.description: Three independent MCMC analyses of 10 7 steps were performed, sampling every 10,000th generation with the burn-in set at 10 6 generations.
steps.searchable_summary: Run MCMC analyses in BEAST with specified parameters

steps.description: To calibrate the molecular clock, we used the same split as described by Jesse et al. (2009), i.e. the one between Potamon fluviatile and P. algeriense, which most likely took place at the end of the Messinian salinity crisis (Brandis et al. 2000).
steps.searchable_summary: Calibrate molecular clock using a known divergence time

steps.description: Past population demography was inferred using a Bayesian skyline plot model (Drummond et al. 2005), as implemented in BEAST v. 1.8.0 (Drummond et al. 2012) and visualised in TRACER v. 1.6 (Rambaut et al. 2014).
steps.searchable_summary: Visualize Bayesian skyline plot results using TRACER
